pyspark_transformation:pyspark_transform.name: pyspark_transformation_Create_table_Create_a_full_denormalized_table_by_joining_all_model_
pyspark_transformation:pyspark_transform.request:
Create table: Create a full denormalized table by joining all model tables.  Use Sales as base table. Join Product on ProductKey, Region on SalesTerritoryKey,  SalesPerson on EmployeeKey, SalesPersonRegion on EmployeeKey, Targets on EmployeeID. Keep all Sales rows with left joins.

=== SCHEMA INFORMATION ===

TABLE STRUCTURES:
  • Sales: ﻿EmployeeKey, Year, Quarter, Month, Day, SalesOrderNumber, SalesTerritoryKey, Sum of Cost, ProductKey, Sum of Quantity, Sum of Sales, Sum of Unit Price, ResellerKey
  • Targets: ﻿EmployeeID, Sum of Target, Year, Quarter, Month, Day
  • SalesPersonRegion: ﻿EmployeeKey, Count of SalesTerritoryKey
  • SalesPerson: ﻿EmployeeID, EmployeeKey, Salesperson, Title, UPN
  • Region: ﻿Country, Group, Region, SalesTerritoryKey
  • Product: ﻿Background Color Format, Category, Color, Font Color Format, Product, ProductKey, Standard Cost, Subcategory

RELATIONSHIP MAP (for joining):
  • Targets[EmployeeID] joins SalesPerson[EmployeeID]
  • Sales[EmployeeKey] joins SalesPersonRegion[EmployeeKey]
  • Sales[SalesTerritoryKey] joins Region[SalesTerritoryKey]
  • Sales[ProductKey] joins Product[ProductKey]

=== COLUMN DEFINITIONS (what each column represents) ===

Sales:
    • EmployeeKey: ID/KEY (for joins)
    • Year: DATE/TIME
    • Quarter: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE
    • Month: DATE/TIME
    • Day: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE
    • SalesOrderNumber: NUMERIC (monetary value)
    • SalesTerritoryKey: NUMERIC (monetary value)
    • Sum of Cost: NUMERIC (monetary value)
    • ProductKey: ID/KEY (for joins)
    • Sum of Quantity: NUMERIC (count/quantity)
    • Sum of Sales: NUMERIC (monetary value)
    • Sum of Unit Price: NUMERIC (monetary value)
    • ResellerKey: ID/KEY (for joins)

Targets:
    • EmployeeID: ID/KEY (for joins)
    • Sum of Target: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE
    • Year: DATE/TIME
    • Quarter: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE
    • Month: DATE/TIME
    • Day: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE

SalesPersonRegion:
    • EmployeeKey: ID/KEY (for joins)
    • Count of SalesTerritoryKey: NUMERIC (monetary value)

SalesPerson:
    • EmployeeID: ID/KEY (for joins)
    • EmployeeKey: ID/KEY (for joins)
    • Salesperson: NUMERIC (monetary value)
    • Title: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE
    • UPN: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE

Region:
    • Country: NUMERIC (count/quantity)
    • Group: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE
    • Region: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE
    • SalesTerritoryKey: NUMERIC (monetary value)

Product:
    • Background Color Format: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE
    • Category: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE
    • Color: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE
    • Font Color Format: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE
    • Product: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE
    • ProductKey: ID/KEY (for joins)
    • Standard Cost: NUMERIC (monetary value)
    • Subcategory: TEXT/ATTRIBUTE

=== COMMON DAX METRIC PATTERNS (examples for your columns) ===
Total/Sum: SUM(Sales[SalesAmount])
Average: AVERAGE(Sales[SalesAmount])
Count of Orders: DISTINCTCOUNT(Sales[OrderID])
Average Order Value: DIVIDE(SUM(Sales[SalesAmount]), DISTINCTCOUNT(Sales[OrderID]))
Sales by Product: CALCULATE([Total Sales], FILTER(Product, Product[ProductKey] = SELECTEDVALUE(Product[ProductKey])))
Profit Margin: DIVIDE(SUM(Sales[SalesAmount]) - SUM(Sales[ProductCost]), SUM(Sales[SalesAmount]))

JOIN PATTERNS:
  • Use LEFT JOINs to preserve all fact table records
  • Join through intermediate tables (e.g., through SalesPerson to reach SalesPersonRegion)
  • Always qualify column names: df['ColumnName'] == OtherTable['ColumnName']
  • Include clear comments for each join explaining its purpose

=== OUTPUT REQUIREMENTS ===
For PySpark denormalized table joins:
  1. Start with base table (Sales recommended)
  2. Join each related table using LEFT JOIN to preserve rows
  3. For multi-hop relationships, join through intermediate tables
  4. Include clear comments above each join explaining the relationship
  5. Create final temp view with meaningful name
  6. Use proper column escaping for special characters


=== CRITICAL INSTRUCTIONS ===
• DO use metric patterns above as templates
• DO use the exact column names from the COLUMN DEFINITIONS section
• DO keep the code language consistent: PySpark
• DO NOT create columns that are not explicitly requested
• DO NOT use columns that are not listed in the schema
• RETURN ONLY the code, no explanation

dax_measure:dax_measure_you_are_a_dax_co.name: dax_measure_YOU_ARE_A_DAX_CODE_GENERATION_EXPERT_YOUR_TASK_Create_a_DAX_measure_NA
dax_measure:dax_measure_you_are_a_dax_co.request:
YOU ARE A DAX CODE GENERATION EXPERT.

YOUR TASK:
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Create a DAX measure NAMED "testMeasure12"

REQUEST: "Create a measure which will return yes if Year = 2019 else No where Sales > 1000"


TARGET ENVIRONMENT: Semantic Model
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

SCHEMA YOU HAVE ACCESS TO:
AVAILABLE TABLES AND COLUMNS:
  Sales: ﻿EmployeeKey, Year, Quarter, Month, Day, SalesOrderNumber, SalesTerritoryKey, Sum of Cost, ProductKey, Sum of Quantity, Sum of Sales, Sum of Unit Price, ResellerKey
  Targets: ﻿EmployeeID, Sum of Target, Year, Quarter, Month, Day
  SalesPersonRegion: ﻿EmployeeKey, Count of SalesTerritoryKey
  SalesPerson: ﻿EmployeeID, EmployeeKey, Salesperson, Title, UPN
  Region: ﻿Country, Group, Region, SalesTerritoryKey
  Product: ﻿Background Color Format, Category, Color, Font Color Format, Product, ProductKey, Standard Cost, Subcategory

RELATIONSHIP MAP (join using these):
  Targets[EmployeeID] → SalesPerson[EmployeeID]
  Sales[EmployeeKey] → SalesPersonRegion[EmployeeKey]
  Sales[SalesTerritoryKey] → Region[SalesTerritoryKey]
  Sales[ProductKey] → Product[ProductKey]


RULES:
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
✓ ONLY use tables and columns listed above
✓ ONLY use column names EXACTLY as shown (case-sensitive with special chars)
✓ For DAX: Use proper DAX syntax
✓ NEVER invent tables or columns
✓ NEVER average or sum ID/Key columns  
✓ For measures: Use IF, AND, OR, CALCULATE as needed
✓ Include ALL logic needed to match the request and conditions

OUTPUT FORMAT:
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Return ONLY the complete, valid DAX code.
For a measure named "testMeasure12", return the full measure definition.
For SQL/PySpark, return the complete query.
NO explanations. NO markdown. Only the code.

NOW GENERATE THE DAX measure:

dax_measure:testmeasure13.name: testMeasure13
dax_measure:testmeasure13.code:
TYPE: DAX
CODE: 
```
testMeasure13 = IF('Sales'[Year] = "2019" && 'Sales'[Sum of Sales] > 1000, "yes", "no")
```
EXPLANATION: This DAX code defines a measure named "testMeasure13" that checks if the 'Year' column in the 'Sales' table is equal to "2019" and if the 'Sum of Sales' column in the 'Sales' table is greater than 1000. If both conditions are met, it returns "yes", otherwise it returns "no". This code matches the user's intent to create a measure that returns "yes" if the year is 2019 and sales are greater than 1000, and "no" otherwise. The code uses the 'Sales' table and the 'Year' and 'Sum of Sales' columns, as specified in the schema.
dax_measure:testmeasure13.request: Create measure named testMeasure13. "Create a measure which will return yes if Year = 2019 else No where Sales > 1000"

pyspark_transformation:testtable13.name: testTable13
pyspark_transformation:testtable13.code:
TYPE: PySpark
CODE: 
```python
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

# Create a SparkSession
spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("testTable13").getOrCreate()

# Load the tables
sales = spark.read.parquet("Sales")
targets = spark.read.parquet("Targets")
sales_person_region = spark.read.parquet("SalesPersonRegion")
sales_person = spark.read.parquet("SalesPerson")
region = spark.read.parquet("Region")
product = spark.read.parquet("Product")

# Join the tables
testTable13 = sales.join(sales_person_region, sales["﻿EmployeeKey"] == sales_person_region["﻿EmployeeKey"]) \
                    .join(sales_person, sales["﻿EmployeeKey"] == sales_person["EmployeeKey"]) \
                    .join(region, sales["SalesTerritoryKey"] == region["SalesTerritoryKey"]) \
                    .join(product, sales["ProductKey"] == product["ProductKey"]) \
                    .join(targets, sales["﻿EmployeeKey"] == targets["﻿EmployeeID"])

# Write the resulting table to a new parquet file
testTable13.write.parquet("testTable13")
```
EXPLANATION: This PySpark code creates a new table named "testTable13" by joining all the available tables. It starts by loading each table into a Spark DataFrame, then joins them together based on their common columns. The resulting table contains all the columns from the original tables. This code matches the user's intent to create a table combining all of the other tables, and it uses only the tables and columns specified in the schema. The code uses the SparkSession to read and write parquet files, and it assumes that the tables are stored in parquet format. The resulting table is written to a new parquet file named "testTable13".
pyspark_transformation:testtable13.request: Create table named testTable13. Create a table combining all of other tables
